title: Files & Exceptions: Dealing with Errors
beginnerExplanation: Real programs need to read data from files and handle the unexpected errors that come with it. Python's open() function reads and writes files, while try/except lets your program recover gracefully when things go wrong instead of crashing.
intermediateExplanation: Data lives in files — text files, CSVs, logs. Your code will encounter missing files, permission errors, and corrupted data. Without exception handling, a single error kills your entire program. With try/except, you catch errors and decide what to do next.

realWorldAnalogy.title: Opening a Locked Chest
realWorldAnalogy.description: Reading a file is like opening a treasure chest. Sometimes the chest is there and opens fine (success). Sometimes it is missing (FileNotFoundError). Sometimes it is locked (PermissionError). Exception handling is your set of lockpicks and backup plans.

mentalModel: Reading a file is like opening a treasure chest. Sometimes the chest is there and opens fine (success). Sometimes it is missing (FileNotFoundError). Sometimes it is locked (PermissionError). Exception handling is your set of lockpicks and backup plans.

executionWalkthrough: 2. Scopes and local namespaces are verified.
3. Target variables are allocated or updated.
commonMistakes: Forgetting to close a file after opening it (the with statement prevents this).
Using "w" mode when you meant "r" — "w" erases the file contents immediately!
Catching all exceptions with bare except: which hides real bugs.
Not handling the encoding — use open("file.txt", encoding="utf-8") for special characters.

debuggingMission.scenario: An adventurer is trying to use Files & Exceptions: Dealing with Errors but hit a common pitfall: Forgetting to close a file after opening it (the with statement prevents this).
debuggingMission.explanation: Make sure to follow the best practices: Always use the with statement — it automatically closes the file even if an error occurs.

practiceQuestions.question: What happens if you open a file with mode "w" that already has data?
options: It appends new data to the end
It erases all existing data first
practiceQuestions.explanation: Mode "w" (write) truncates the file to zero length first, effectively erasing all existing content before writing new data.

practiceQuestions.question: Why is "with open()" preferred over just "open()"?
options: It automatically closes the file when done
It reads the whole file at once
practiceQuestions.explanation: The with statement acts as a context manager that guarantees the file is properly closed when you exit the block, even if an exception occurs.

bossBattle.name: The Corruption Worm
bossBattle.description: A vile worm that corrupts your data files mid-read, injecting bad lines and missing delimiters. You must write bulletproof parsing code to survive.
bossBattle.mechanic: Parse a corrupted data file using try/except to skip bad lines and extract all valid records without crashing.

artifacts.name: The Context Shield
artifacts.description: A magical shield (the "with" statement) that automatically seals file connections when danger strikes.

developerMode.industryUsage: Files & Exceptions: Dealing with Errors is widely used in standard library scripts and enterprise Python programs.
developerMode.performanceConsiderations: Optimized by standard compiler rules in modern Python environments.
bestPractices: Always use the with statement — it automatically closes the file even if an error occurs.
Catch specific exceptions (FileNotFoundError, ValueError) not bare except.
Use .strip() when reading lines to remove trailing newlines.
Use .split() to break lines into parts when reading structured data like CSV.
antiPatterns: Forgetting to close a file after opening it (the with statement prevents this).
Using "w" mode when you meant "r" — "w" erases the file contents immediately!
Catching all exceptions with bare except: which hides real bugs.
Not handling the encoding — use open("file.txt", encoding="utf-8") for special characters.
